notes: Jeremiah, like Ezekiel, was a priest (Jer. 1:1; Ezekiel 1:3). Anathoth was one of thirteen cities appointed by Joshua for the priests of Aaron among the forty-eight cities designated for the Levites (Joshua 21:18; BD, "Levites"; "Priests"; "Aaronic Priesthood"). (Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 541.)

Anathoth "answers to prayer" Anathoth, a little town in the land of Benjamin, lies three miles northeast of Jerusalem and may be viewed from Mount Scopus at the north end of the Mount of Olives. (Nyman, 1.)

Each prophet can trace the origin of his prophetic calling to a specific encounter in which the Lord called him as his representative and revealed the nature of his prophetic mission. Prophets are frequently called in the course of their normal daily lives: Enoch was journeying in the land (Moses 6:26), Moses was herding sheep (Ex. 3:1), Samuel was asleep in his bed (1 Sam. 3:3), Amos was following his flocks (Amos 7:15), and Ezekiel was sitting beside the river Chebar (Ezek. 1:3). Most expressed humility and some a certain reluctance to undertake a lifetime calling that would cause them to be scorned and abused by men and require them to exercise faith beyond the capacities of most mortals. Jeremiah simply noted, "The word of the Lord came unto me" (Jer. 1:4). The Lord introduced Jeremiah's call with an extraordinary revelation of his premortal preparation and foreordination for such a calling: "Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee; and before thou camest forth out of the womb I sanctified thee, and I ordained thee a prophet" (Jer. 1:5). Like Enoch, Moses, and Isaiah before him, Jeremiah expressed his inadequacy: "Ah, Lord God! behold, I cannot speak: for I am a child" (Jer. 1:6). To Enoch's reply that he was young, hated, and slow of speech, the Lord had promised, "Open thy mouth, and it shall be filled, and I will give thee utterance" (Moses 6:32; see also 6:33-36). To Moses' complaint that he too was "slow of speech," the Lord had promised that he would be with his mouth but finally promised in addition to provide for him a spokesman, his brother Aaron (Ex. 4:10-16). Isaiah felt unworthy to be in the presence of the Lord and answered, "I am a man of unclean lips" -to which the Lord sent a seraph with a live coal from the altar to touch Isaiah's mouth and promise, "thine iniquity is taken away, and thy sin purged" (Isa. 6:5-7). Just as he had to Enoch, Moses, and Isaiah, the Lord responded to Jeremiah with a promise followed by a distinctive symbolic act. He said, "Say not, I am a child: for thou shalt go to all that I shall send thee, and whatsoever I command thee thou shalt speak. Be not afraid of their faces: for I am with thee to deliver thee, saith the Lord" (Jer. 1:7-8). With that admonition and promise, the Lord, in the words of Jeremiah, "put forth his hand, and touched my mouth. And the Lord said unto me, Behold, I have put my words in thy mouth" (Jer. 1:9). This gesture would serve Jeremiah throughout his mission as a sober reminder of the divine origin and urgency of the words that he would be asked to deliver and as a comfort that the Lord was with him to deliver him from his enemies. It also serves to remind us that the words in his book are of God. The Lord then designated Jeremiah's far-reaching audience as well as the essential outline of his message: "See, I have this day set thee over the nations and over the kingdoms, to root out, and to pull down, and to destroy, and to throw down, to build, and to plant" (Jer. 1:10). Jeremiah was called to be a prophet to the nations, and his mission was to proclaim destruction as well as restoration. Obviously the prophecies of doom would make Jeremiah unpopular and would lead to much persecution, even to the point of threatening his life. Yet the Lord promised that he would protect him: "For, behold, I have made thee this day a defenced city, and an iron pillar, and brasen walls against the whole land" (Jer. 1:18). This imagery is ironic, for though Jeremiah would be protected as a mighty city, his people and the holy city they inhabited would not be. But protection from his enemies did not lessen the burden of suffering that Jeremiah would be called upon to bear. (David Rolph Seely in Kent P. Jackson, ed., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 4: 1 Kings to Malachi, p.195)

Let us not lose sight of the fact that all through the history of Israel until the coming of our Redeemer, the blessings of the Holy Priesthood were restricted. It was not given universally to the tribes, but of necessity there had to be some faithful men upon whom the Melchizedek Priesthood was conferred. All of the prophets held the Melchizedek Priesthood, but the Prophet Joseph Smith has informed us that in each case it was by special divine appointment. There was never a time in Israel when there was not a prophet with divine authority with power to confirm and perform other ordinances. . . . So other prophets like Isaiah, Jeremiah. Ezekiel, and Daniel were blessed with the Melchizedek Priesthood. They could officiate among the people. but there was no universal bestowal of authority among the tribes, from the time of the entrance of Israel into the promised land, until the coming of our Savior. When he came, the fulness of the gospel and of divine authority was restored. (Joseph Fielding Smith, Answers to Gospel Questions., 5 vols. (Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Company, 1979), 4:159-160.)

The word of the Lord came also to Jeremiah the prophet saying that before he was born the Lord "sanctified (him) and ordained (him) a prophet unto the nations." (Jeremiah 1:5.) These and other similar scriptures make it plain that man existed in a spiritual condition before coming here to this earth and that in that pre-existence he had his free agency or right to choose. How could some of those spirits as seen by Abraham have become noble and great except by faithfulness to God's cause? How came God to choose Abraham or Jeremiah before they were born? If we had the complete answer to these questions, we would no doubt learn that they and others stood up valiantly with Michael and his angels when "there was war in heaven. . . . and Satan and his angels were cast out" of heaven for rebellion. (Rev. 12:7-12.) (Harold B. Lee, Decisions for Successful Living, p.163-164)

Joseph Smith was ordained before he came here, the same as Jeremiah was. Said the Lord unto him, "Before you were begotten I knew you," etc. So do I believe with regard to this people, so do I believe with regard to the apostles, the high priests, seventies and the elders of Israel bearing the Holy Priesthood, I believe they were ordained before they came here; and I believe the God of Israel has raised them up and has watched over them from their youth, has carried them through all the scenes of life both seen and unseen, and has prepared them as instruments in His hands to take this kingdom and bear it off. If this be so, what manner of men ought we to be? If anything under the heavens should humble men before the Lord and before one another, it should be the fact that we have been called of God. (Wilford Woodruff, Journal of Discourses 21:317, October 10, 1880)

This passage is one of the few clear revelations about foreordination in the scriptures. It tells of Jeremiah's being sanctified for special service and ordained to be a prophet in his premortal life-in the spirit world, for it happened before his body was even formed (Jer. 1:5 and fn.). Nevertheless, like Moses and Enoch before him, Jeremiah felt inadequate for his calling (Jer. 1:6). The Lord assured him he would be able to go where divinely sent and speak what he would be commanded to speak, but he must not be afraid. The Lord touched Jeremiah's mouth and assigned him six difficult tasks as a reformer (Jer. 1:6-10 and fn.). (Ellis Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 541.)

quotes.text: (Ellis Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 541.) Every man who has a calling to minister to the inhabitants of the world was ordained to that very purpose in the Grand Council of heaven before this world was. I suppose I was ordained to this very office in that Grand Council. It is the testimony that I want that I am God's servant, and this people His people.
quotes.attr: Joseph Smith, Teachings of the Prophet Joseph Smith, 365.

quotes.text: and Satan and his angels were cast out" of heaven for rebellion. (Rev. 12:7-12.)

notes: This verse is often cited to show that Jeremiah was very young at the time of his call. Some have suggested that he was near the age of Josiah, the king, during whose reign he commenced his ministry. Josiah was about eight years old when he began to reign; Jeremiah's ministry began in the thirteenth year of Josiah, so this would make Jeremiah around twenty years old at the time of his call. Thus he would hardly have been a "child," but it is possible that Jeremiah's assessment of himself as a child may have been a relative thing. (Enoch considered himself a "lad" when he was sixty-five years old-see Moses 6:31.) Although Jeremiah's exact age is neither determinable nor important, there is some significance to his being called at an early age. It fits a pattern throughout the ages of the Lord's calling his prophets in their early years. Samuel was called while he was actually a child. Although Moses was called when eighty, he had been literally raised up by the Lord for those eighty years and prepared to lead the children of Israel out of Egypt (see Exodus, chapters 2 and 3, and Hebrews 11:23-27). Nephi was exceedingly young when he was visited by the Lord and informed that he would become a ruler and teacher over his brethren (see 1 Nephi 2:16-24), Mormon was fifteen years of age when he was visited by the Lord and became the Lord's special witness to the Nephites in their final years (see Mormon 1:15). And, of course, Joseph Smith was only in his fifteenth year when he experienced his "First Vision" (see Joseph Smith-History 1:7-20). The reason for calling prophets in their youth was clarified by the Savior in his teachings about not sewing new cloth on an old garment or putting new wine in old bottles (see Mark 2:21-22). When his church was not upon the earth. the Lord often called his prophets while they were young so that he could train and prepare them before they were corrupted by the teachings of the apostate world in which they were living. Some may point to the age of our prophets, seers, and revelators of this dispensation as being inconsistent with this principle, but it should be remembered that these great, aged prophets have experienced a lifetime of service and preparation in local positions of authority and as General Authorities before being called to preside over the Church. (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah, p.17-18)

inadequate, frightened. "Ah, Lord," he cried, "behold, I cannot speak: for I am [but] a child." But the Lord reassured him: "Be not afraid of their faces: for I am with thee. … Therefore gird up thy loins, and arise, and speak unto them." So speak unto them he did, but initially not with much success. Things went from bad to worse until finally he was imprisoned and made a laughingstock among the people. Angry that he had been so mistreated and maligned, Jeremiah vowed, in effect, never to teach another lesson, whether that be to an investigator, Primary child, new convert, or-heaven forbid-the 15-year-olds. "I will not make mention of [the Lord], nor speak any more in his name," the discouraged prophet said. But then came the turning point of Jeremiah's life. Something had been happening with every testimony he had borne, every scripture he had read, every truth he had taught. Something had been happening that he hadn't counted on. Even as he vowed to close his mouth and walk away from the Lord's work, he found that he could not. Why? Because "his word was in mine heart as a burning fire shut up in my bones, and I was weary with forbearing, and I could not stay." (Jeffrey R. Holland, in Conference Report, Apr. 1998, 33; or Ensign, May 1998, 27)
A memorable account of the power of such teaching comes from the life of the prophet Jeremiah. This great man felt the way most teachers or speakers or Church officers feel when called-inexperienced,

And, of course, Joseph Smith was only in his fifteenth year when he experienced his "First Vision"

notes: from harm is also consistent with the Lord's promises to other prophets. Similar promises were extended to Moses and Joshua (see Exodus 4:10-17; Joshua 1:1-9). The Lord assured Paul of this as he labored in Corinth (see Acts 18:6-11). Nephi, the son of Helaman, declared what the Lord told him to declare and was "taken by the Spirit and conveyed away" from those who sought to cast him into prison (see Helaman 10:11-17). And Nephi, the son of Nephi, was given greater power than those who had sought to stone the prophets, with the result that "it were not possible that they could disbelieve his words" (3 Nephi 7:14-20). Jeremiah apparently did not always remember this promise, but, as will be shown, it was fulfilled in his life. (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah, p.18)
The Lord's comforting assurance that he would be with Jeremiah in his speaking and would deliver him

The touching of Jeremiah's mouth was not done for the same reason as was the touching of Isaiah's mouth with a hot coal during his call (see Isaiah 6:6-7). Isaiah's was a symbolic representation of his being forgiven of his sins, while Jeremiah's was a confirmation of the promise recorded in verse 7 that he would speak whatsoever the Lord commanded him. It should not be assumed that Jeremiah was left unforgiven of his sins, however. A call to the ministry would carry with it a promise of forgiveness of past sins, at least on a conditional basis (see D&C 82:1-7); this is the doctrine of justification. (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah, p.18)

The charge "to root out, and to pull down, and to destroy" is also referred to at other times in Jeremiah's prophecies (see 12:17; 18:7). Jeremiah's mission was to all the nations and kingdoms, not just Judah. In carrying out his mission, he prophesied against all the surrounding nations as well as against Judah (see chapters 46-51 of Jeremiah and chapter 15 of this work). The charge "to root out, and to pull down, and to destroy" was a charge to testify against the evils of the day and to call the people to repentance; this Jeremiah did throughout his ministry. His mission was also "to build, and to plant." The captives who were taken into the land of the Chaldeans were to return and be built and planted (see 24:6). Those who were to be restored in the latter-days were likewise to be built and planted (see 31:28). Following the destruction of Jerusalem by Nebuchadnezzar, those who were allowed to remain in the land were promised that if they did remain they would be built up and not pulled down, they would be planted and not plucked out (see 42:10). However, they rejected this opportunity and went into Egypt, where they were pulled down and destroyed. Jeremiah's call might be compared to Joseph Smith's call to raise a voice of warning to all the inhabitants of the earth (see D&C 1). and to the angel Moroni's declaration to Joseph "that God had a work for me [Joseph] to do; and that my name should be had for good and evil among all nations, kindreds, and tongues, or that it should be both good and evil spoken of among all people" (Joseph Smith-History 1:33). (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah , p.18-19) 1:11-12. The meaning of the vision of the almond tree is uncertain. Because the almond tree is the first tree to blossom in the spring, some commentators emphasize that this is a watchful twig, the first to open its eyes. Others emphasize that the vision shows, not a flowering branch, but a rod which is to be used as a stick to beat with, or a rod of correction. Others emphasize a wordplay between the Hebrew words for "almond" and "watching," showing by association that Jehovah is watching over his word to bring it to pass. Until further knowledge of "the manner of prophesying among the Jews" is given (see 2
Nephi 25:1-2), the interpretation of the almond rod cannot be definitely ascertained. ). (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah , p.19)
set thee over--literally, "appointed thee to the oversight." (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

quotes.text: and destroyed. Jeremiah's call might be compared to Joseph Smith's call to raise a voice of warning to all the inhabitants of the earth (see D&C 1). and to the angel Moroni's declaration to Joseph "that God had a work for me [Joseph] to do; and that my name should be had for good and evil among all nations, kindreds, and tongues, or that it should be both good and evil spoken of among all people"

notes: As a reminder that the Lord would fulfill Jeremiah's prophecies, the almond tree was given as a symbol; the Hebrew letters of its name (dqv shqd) are the same letters as the word for "be alert, wakeful"; thus whenever Jeremiah saw that tree (which is the earliest tree to bloom in Israel), he would be reminded of the promise that the Lord would be attentive to his prophecies and fulfill his words (Jer. 1:11-12; BD, "Almond"). (Ellis Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 541)

almond tree--literally, "the wakeful tree," because it awakes from the sleep of winter earlier than the other trees, flowering in January, and bearing fruit in March; [this is the time of passover] symbol of God's early execution of His purpose; Jer 1:12, "hasten My word" (compare Am 8:3). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

An almond branch was evidently chosen because it is the first tree to bud in spring. As the almond tree hastens to come into blossom, so would the word of the Lord through Jeremiah hasten to fulfillment. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 236)
rod (maqqel) from an unused root meaning apparently to germinate

13-16. The interpretation of the seething pot seems to be easier. The pot is seething, about to boil over. The Lord tells Jeremiah that an evil from the north is about to come upon the inhabitants of the land. This evil is apparently the nation of Babylon, whom the Lord will allow to come and thus bring his Judgments upon the wickedness of Judah. Babylon is to play the same role with Judah that Assyria played with northern Israel: The wicked will punish the wicked (see Mormon 4:5). Some scholars question whether these verses or visions were a part of the original call, claiming that they were given later and inserted into the text because the siege and destruction by Babylon came many years later. This is a reiection of prophecy. We should remember that the Lord knows the end from the beginning, and he is here outlining all of Jeremiah's mission. The stage was already set by the people's wickedness, although it was years before the prophecy would be fulfilled. (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah, p.19)

Next, the vision of a "seething pot" was shown to Jeremiah, symbolizing the disaster and pain which, like the contents of a boiling cauldron, would spill over and run down the kingdoms of the north to overwhelm Judah (see C. F. Keil and F. Delitzsch, Commentary on the Old Testament, 8:1:43–44). (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 236)
north (tsaphown) from tsaphan "to lie hidden, lurk" and "to hide, hide from discovery"

The burning of incense (see Jeremiah 1:16) is a symbol of prayer (see Revelation 5:8; 8:3). Far more is implied in the Lord's accusation than just a ritual of burning incense to false gods. The people were seeking help and guidance from the false gods rather than from the Lord. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 236)
prevail (yakol) to prevail, overcome, endure, have power, be able
